---
title: "Decision Trace Reconstructor — Evaluation Report"
subtitle: "Decision Trace Reconstructor v0.1.0 — Reference Implementation of DEMM"
author:
  - name: Oleg Solozobov
    orcid: 0009-0009-0105-7459
date: 2026-04-28
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
abstract: |
  This report renders the canonical evaluation results of the
  Decision Trace Reconstructor (v0.1.0) — the reference
  implementation of the Decision Evidence Maturity Model (DEMM)
  property-level reconstructability assessment. Results cover a
  140-scenario synthetic evaluation across a 3 × 2 architecture
  × stack-coverage matrix plus a non-agentic baseline, and three
  named-incident reconstructions admitted under the
  *public-record evidence / method inference* convention.
  The numbers below are read directly from `results/*.json` at
  render time and are the release-pinned evaluation artifacts
  for this software version.
keywords:
  - decision trace reconstructor
  - DEMM
  - decision trace reconstruction
  - agentic AI governance
  - W3C PROV-O
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
    number-sections: true
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 6
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fig-width: 6
    fig-height: 4
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  freeze: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
import json
from pathlib import Path

RESULTS = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "results" if "__file__" in dir() else Path("../results")
# Fallback for Quarto execution context (cwd = docs/)
if not RESULTS.exists():
    RESULTS = Path("../results")

cells = json.loads((RESULTS / "cells.json").read_text())
per_property = json.loads((RESULTS / "per_property.json").read_text())
named_incidents = json.loads((RESULTS / "named_incidents.json").read_text())
per_scenario = json.loads((RESULTS / "per_scenario.json").read_text())

ARCH_LABELS = {
    "single_agent": "Single-agent tool-use",
    "multi_agent": "Multi-agent orchestration",
    "human_in_the_loop": "Human-in-the-loop agentic",
    "non_agentic": "Non-agentic baseline",
}
TIER_LABELS = {
    "within_stack": "Within-stack",
    "cross_stack": "Cross-stack",
    "baseline": "Baseline",
}
ARCH_ORDER = ["single_agent", "multi_agent", "human_in_the_loop", "non_agentic"]
TIER_ORDER = ["within_stack", "cross_stack", "baseline"]

cells_index = {(c["architecture"], c["stack_tier"]): c for c in cells}


def md_table(headers: list[str], rows: list[list[str]]) -> str:
    """Render a Markdown table; returns the source string."""
    head = "| " + " | ".join(headers) + " |"
    sep = "|" + "|".join("---" for _ in headers) + "|"
    body = "\n".join("| " + " | ".join(r) + " |" for r in rows)
    return "\n".join([head, sep, body])
```

# Introduction

The reconstructor is a measurement instrument. It takes stored evidence fragments produced
during the execution of an agentic AI system — agent logs, tool-call records,
intermediate outputs, policy snapshots — and reconstructs the decision chain
post-hoc, projecting each step onto the canonical *Decision Event
Schema* and classifying every property as `fully_fillable`,
`partially_fillable`, `structurally_unfillable`, or `opaque`. The per-property
feasibility tensor is the input to the five-level **Decision Evidence
Maturity Model (DEMM)** rubric implemented by this package. Companion
method publications are in preparation and are not cited until public
identifiers exist.

The pipeline is six stages: fragment collection, temporal ordering, chain
assembly, decision-boundary detection (four heuristics), decision-event schema
mapping, and feasibility-report generation. ML-opaque reasoning steps are
not "reconstructed" — the reconstructor substitutes an authorization envelope (inputs
available, constraints active, outputs possible).

This report renders the evaluation results from `results/*.json`. The
numbers below are produced at render time; if a result file changes, this
report changes with it.

# Evaluation matrix

The reconstructor is evaluated on a 3 × 2 × baseline grid: three agentic architectures
(single-agent tool-use, multi-agent orchestration, human-in-the-loop
agentic) crossed with two stack-coverage tiers (within-stack governed
runtimes, cross-stack boundaries), anchored by a non-agentic baseline.
Twenty synthetic scenarios per cell, deterministic-given-seed, plus 20
non-agentic baseline scenarios = **140 total scenarios**.

| Architecture | Within-stack | Cross-stack |
|---|---|---|
| Single-agent tool-use | AgentCore / LangGraph + LangSmith | Agent + external MCP / browser tools |
| Multi-agent orchestration | LangGraph multi-agent / OpenAI Agents SDK | MCP-mediated multi-agent |
| Human-in-the-loop agentic | Approval-gated HITL runtime | HITL with external tool execution |
| Non-agentic baseline | Discrete rule + model system with explicit decision point | — |

: Evaluation matrix: architectures × stack-coverage tiers with representative
systems per cell. {#tbl-matrix}

# Results

## Reconstruction completeness per cell

```{python}
#| label: tbl-completeness-build
def fmt_cell(c):
    if c is None:
        return "n/a"
    return f"{c['completeness_pct']:.1f}% [{c['completeness_ci_low']:.1f}, {c['completeness_ci_high']:.1f}]"

rows = []
for arch in ARCH_ORDER[:-1]:
    rows.append([
        ARCH_LABELS[arch],
        fmt_cell(cells_index.get((arch, "within_stack"))),
        fmt_cell(cells_index.get((arch, "cross_stack"))),
    ])
baseline = cells_index.get(("non_agentic", "within_stack"))
rows.append([ARCH_LABELS["non_agentic"], fmt_cell(baseline), "—"])
table_md = md_table(["Architecture", "Within-stack", "Cross-stack"], rows)
```

```{python}
#| output: asis
print(table_md + "\n\n: Reconstruction completeness, mean % [95 % CI bootstrap]. {#tbl-completeness}\n")
```

The non-agentic baseline (@tbl-completeness, last row) anchors the
upper bound of property-level reconstructability available when the subject
system is maximally instrumented by design. The agentic cells degrade
relative to this anchor — the magnitude and pattern of the degradation are
what the rest of the report is about.

## Boundary-detection F1

```{python}
#| output: asis
rows = []
for arch in ARCH_ORDER[:-1]:
    rows.append([
        ARCH_LABELS[arch],
        f"{cells_index[(arch, 'within_stack')]['boundary_f1']:.3f}",
        f"{cells_index[(arch, 'cross_stack')]['boundary_f1']:.3f}",
    ])
baseline_f1 = cells_index.get(("non_agentic", "within_stack"), {}).get("boundary_f1", 0.0)
rows.append([ARCH_LABELS["non_agentic"], f"{baseline_f1:.3f}", "—"])
print(md_table(["Architecture", "Within-stack F1", "Cross-stack F1"], rows))
print("\n: Boundary-detection F1 vs ground-truth synthetic boundaries. {#tbl-boundary-f1}\n")
```

Boundary F1 is a mechanistic diagnostic: it separates *boundary error*
(the reconstructor partitioning the chain wrong) from *evidence gap* (the property
genuinely missing). Cross-stack cells have lower F1 because cross-stack
boundaries are exactly where heuristics fire less reliably.

## 7-mode operational-failure dominance

```{python}
#| output: asis
rows = []
for arch in ARCH_ORDER[:-1]:
    for tier in ("within_stack", "cross_stack"):
        c = cells_index.get((arch, tier))
        if not c:
            continue
        mode = c["modal_mode"] if c["modal_mode"] is not None else "—"
        share = f"{c['modal_mode_share']:.2f}" if c["modal_mode_share"] is not None else "—"
        brk = c["dominant_break"] or "—"
        rows.append([ARCH_LABELS[arch], TIER_LABELS[tier], str(mode), share, brk])
print(md_table(
    ["Architecture", "Stack tier", "Modal mode", "Mode share", "Dominant break"],
    rows,
))
print(
    "\n: 7-mode dominance per cell. Modes 1/2/5/6 → decision diffusion; "
    "modes 3/4 → evidence fragmentation; mode 7 → responsibility ambiguity. "
    "{#tbl-7mode}\n",
)
```

The dominant-break attribution maps the seven operational modes onto the
three structural-break labels used by the project:
**decision diffusion** (modes 1, 2, 5, 6), **evidence
fragmentation** (modes 3, 4), and **responsibility ambiguity** (mode 7).

## Per-property feasibility distribution

```{python}
#| output: asis
property_order = [
    "inputs", "policy_basis", "operator_identity", "authorization_envelope",
    "reasoning_trace", "output_action", "post_condition_state",
]
cat_order = ["fully_fillable", "partially_fillable", "structurally_unfillable", "opaque"]
rows = []
for p in property_order:
    if p not in per_property:
        continue
    d = per_property[p]
    rows.append([
        p,
        f"{d.get('fully_fillable', 0):.1f}",
        f"{d.get('partially_fillable', 0):.1f}",
        f"{d.get('structurally_unfillable', 0):.1f}",
        f"{d.get('opaque', 0):.1f}",
    ])
print(md_table(
    ["Property", "Fully", "Partial", "Struct. unfill.", "Opaque"],
    rows,
))
print(
    "\n: Per-property feasibility distribution across all 120 agentic "
    "scenarios (% of property-slots). `reasoning_trace` is 100 % opaque by "
    "design (authorization envelope substituted); `authorization_envelope` is "
    "100 % fully fillable as the substituted surrogate. {#tbl-perprop}\n",
)
```

The two extremes — `reasoning_trace` (100 % opaque) and
`authorization_envelope` (100 % fully fillable) — are not findings but
*specifications*: DEMM substitutes the envelope for the opaque trace, by
construction. The empirical content sits in the middle five rows, where
`policy_basis`, `operator_identity`, `output_action`, and
`post_condition_state` show the actual reconstructability gradients across
the agentic population.

## Named-incident reconstructions

Three named incidents are admitted under a *public-record evidence /
method inference* convention used by this release. Fragments are hand-authored
to reflect what a public-record reader can recover; properties
not disclosed publicly are left un-filled so the reconstructor reports them as
`structurally_unfillable`.

```{python}
#| output: asis
rows = []
for inc in named_incidents:
    counts = inc["feasibility_counts"]
    mode = inc["dominant_mode"] if inc["dominant_mode"] is not None else "—"
    brk = inc["dominant_break"] or "—"
    rows.append([
        inc["incident"],
        ARCH_LABELS.get(inc["architecture"], inc["architecture"]),
        TIER_LABELS.get(inc["stack_tier"], inc["stack_tier"]),
        str(inc["fragments"]),
        str(inc["units_detected"]),
        f"{inc['completeness_pct']:.1f}",
        str(counts["fully_fillable"]),
        str(counts["partially_fillable"]),
        str(counts["structurally_unfillable"]),
        str(counts["opaque"]),
        str(mode),
        brk,
    ])
print(md_table(
    [
        "Incident", "Arch.", "Tier", "Frags", "Units", "Compl. %",
        "Full", "Part.", "Struct.", "Opaque", "Mode", "Break",
    ],
    rows,
))
print(
    "\n: Named-incident reconstruction summary. The recurring 57.1 % "
    "completeness reflects the same per-property gap profile across all "
    "three single-agent cross-stack incidents, consistent with the "
    "synthetic single-agent cross-stack cell (@tbl-completeness). "
    "{#tbl-named-incidents}\n",
)
```

The same per-property gap profile recurs across all three incidents because
the structural failure is the same: a single-agent cross-stack interaction
where the agent's reasoning is opaque, the policy basis is implicit, the
authorization envelope is absent, and the cross-stack boundary discards
inputs and post-conditions to varying degrees. The reconstructor does not need to know
the specific destructive command — it reads the same gap-shaped hole the
public record left behind.

## Severity ranking

The cell-level completeness numbers (@tbl-completeness) admit a severity
ranking by absolute deficit relative to the non-agentic baseline:

```{python}
#| output: asis
baseline_pct = baseline["completeness_pct"] if baseline else 100.0
rows = []
for arch in ARCH_ORDER[:-1]:
    for tier in ("within_stack", "cross_stack"):
        c = cells_index.get((arch, tier))
        if not c:
            continue
        deficit = baseline_pct - c["completeness_pct"]
        rows.append([
            ARCH_LABELS[arch],
            TIER_LABELS[tier],
            f"{c['completeness_pct']:.1f}",
            f"{deficit:.1f}",
            c["dominant_break"] or "—",
        ])
rows.sort(key=lambda r: float(r[3]), reverse=True)
print(md_table(
    ["Architecture", "Tier", "Completeness %", "Deficit vs baseline", "Dominant break"],
    rows,
))
print(
    "\n: Severity ranking by completeness deficit relative to the "
    "non-agentic baseline. The most degraded cell is multi-agent "
    "cross-stack; the least degraded is human-in-the-loop within-stack. "
    "{#tbl-severity}\n",
)
```

# Reproducibility

All synthetic scenarios are deterministic given seeds: same seed, bit-identical
output. The release tag (v0.1.0), the synthetic-generator seed list,
the JSON input manifests per cell, the raw feasibility reports, and
the per-property confidence scores are all in this artifact. To regenerate:

```bash
decision-trace evaluate synthetic --out results/
decision-trace evaluate named --out results/
python scripts/export_schemas.py --out docs/schemas/
quarto render docs/report.qmd
```

The PROV-O JSON-LD siblings (`results/*.jsonld`) carry the same data as
W3C PROV vocabulary and parse with `rdflib` into queryable RDF graphs (see
the SPARQL example in the README).

Named-incident reconstructions are not bit-reproducible in the strong sense
because they depend on public reporting that may update; the fragment
manifests in `src/reconstructor/synthetic/named_incidents.py` pin the
reading of the public record at the time of authorship.

# References

W3C. *PROV-O: The PROV Ontology* (W3C Recommendation, 30 April 2013).
<https://www.w3.org/TR/prov-o/>

Citation File Format. *CFF v1.2.0*. <https://citation-file-format.github.io/>

Research Object Crate. *RO-Crate v1.1*.
<https://www.researchobject.org/ro-crate/1.1/>
